text:
In 2021, the Prosiměřice 2 metal hoard was uncovered in the course of a rescue archaeological excavation that was conducted in the course of drainage works on the northern part of the Jevišovka River floodplain cadastre. The largest assemblage of metallic artifacts discovered to date in the Prosiměřice area was found underwater. The hoard consists of 9 metal ingots and a piece of the sprue that had broken off during their cooling and shaping and 2 socket axes. Chronologically the Prosiměřice 2 hoard belongs to the latter phase of the Middle Danube Urnfield Culture. A comprehensive material analysis of the artifacts points to variations in the applied metallurgical methods including both a high and a low purity copper in the form of metallic ingots, as well as tin bronze for the manufacture of the socket axes, the sprue fragment, and a few of the metal ingot fragments.

Bronze axes from Prosiměřice, South Moravia, are studied using X-ray computed tomography and use-wear analysis, confirming their functionality, while also pointing to some imperfections in their production. The castings show evident casting defects and a high porosity. The Prosiměřice hoards, like the recently described Prosiměřice 1 hoard, contain objects at all levels of the technology of their production. Like the Prosiměřice 1 hoard, the two new hoards are found within an open settlement of the same time period. The intentional disposal of the Prosiměřice hoards in a wetland or subaqueous environment can be considered an act that cannot be reversed. Likewise to other multi-piece hoards intentionally deposited in aquatic environments, we assume that the Prosiměřice hoards were also the focal objects of a communal socio-cultic ritual, and not just abandoned as by-products of the bronze working activities carried out in the nearby open settlement.
